title: Escalation Economics

content.statement: Not all problems deserve senior attention.
content.question: What's your threshold, in dollars or impact, below which someone should just decide without asking you?
sop: Track every decision escalated to you for two weeks and note the dollar or impact size of each.
Find the natural cutoff, the point below which your input rarely changed the outcome.
Set that number as your official threshold and tell your team in writing: below X, decide without me.
Rate Value Preserved, Delay, and Escalation Cost for a recent escalation (see calculation, page 5)
Review escalations quarterly and raise the threshold as your team proves it can be trusted with bigger calls.
content.explainer: Every escalation costs time. Set a dollar threshold under which people must decide without you.
questions: What's your threshold, in dollars or impact, below which someone should just decide without asking you?
What was the last decision someone escalated to you that cost more in delay than it saved in accuracy?
If you set a dollar threshold today, what would you actually lose by letting your team decide under it alone?
content.equation_example: Estimate Value Preserved in dollars, then Delay and Escalation Cost in dollars. Value Preserved $500, Delay cost $50, Escalation Cost $100 → EE = 500 - (50+100) = $350. Higher EE means the escalation was worth it; a low or negative EE means it should have been decided without you.
content.closing_statement: Your attention has a price. Guard it.

caption:
Principle Nº 14: Escalation Economics

Not all problems deserve senior attention. Every escalation costs time.

Ask yourself: What's your threshold, in dollars or impact, below which someone should just decide without asking you?
